summary: McKenzie documents his early discovery of long-tail search traffic for his Bingo Card Creator software business. After obsessing over his lack of ranking for competitive paid keywords, he notices that 20% of his traffic comes from obscure, highly specific organic search queries where his site is one of only a few relevant results in the world. He catalogs the specific long-tail queries from Google and MSN that are sending him free traffic, noting that many align perfectly with his product's niche features. The post captures the moment of realization that targeting the long tail of search can be more valuable than competing for expensive head terms.
key_points: Long-tail search queries with very low competition can drive significant traffic (20% of his total) for free, compared to expensive paid keywords
Creating targeted pages for specific search queries (like 'first grade sight words') is an effective SEO strategy for small businesses
MSN ranked his site much higher than Google for competitive terms, though MSN had far less traffic
Many organic search queries he received were ones he hadn't even thought to bid on, revealing untapped keyword opportunities
The long tail works because for highly specific queries, there may only be a handful of relevant pages in the entire world
money_quotes: They're all from the looooooooooooooooooooong tail of search requests, things for which there are literally perhaps 3 relevant pages in the entire world.
I love SEO — I made a page just to catch this query, and catch it did.
I'm #4 for a key search term after 2 weeks. Not bad. A pity MSN gets so little traffic.
I had not bid for this combination yet. FIXED.
Thats what I pay good money for ($12.50 so far… come on, uISV gods, let me have a sale soon…)
key_insight: For a small software business, the long tail of highly specific search queries can deliver substantial free traffic because there are so few competing pages for each obscure query.
